name: HTML page has lang attribute
description: This rule checks that an HTML page has a non-empty `lang` attribute.
applicability_text:
This rule applies to any [document element](https://dom.spec.whatwg.org/#document-element) if it is an `html` element for which all the following are true:

- is in a [top-level browsing context](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#top-level-browsing-context); and
- has a [node document](https://dom.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-node-document) with a [content type](https://dom.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-document-content-type) of `text/html`.

**Note:** `html` elements within `iframe` and `object` elements are not applicable as `iframe` and `object` elements create [nested browsing contexts](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#nested-browsing-context). However, as these elements are meant to provide a layer of isolation, the declared language of their [parent browsing context](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#parent-browsing-context) will likely not be inherited, making it possible for empty `lang` attributes in [nested browsing contexts](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#nested-browsing-context) to also cause accessibility issues.
expectations: Each test target has a `lang` [attribute value][] that is neither empty (`""`) nor only [ASCII whitespace](https://infra.spec.whatwg.org/#ascii-whitespace).

examples.name: Passed Example 1
examples.description: This `html` element has a `lang` attribute with a non-empty (`""`) value.
examples.code:
<html lang="en">
	<body>
		The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
	</body>
</html>

examples.name: Failed Example 1
examples.description: This `html` element does not have a `lang` attribute.
examples.code:
<html>
	<body>
		The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
	</body>
</html>

examples.name: Failed Example 2
examples.description: This `html` element has a `lang` attribute with an empty (`""`) value.
examples.code:
<html lang="">
	<body>
		The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
	</body>
</html>

examples.name: Failed Example 3
examples.description: This `html` element has a `lang` attribute whose value is only [ASCII whitespace](https://infra.spec.whatwg.org/#ascii-whitespace).
examples.code:
<html lang=" ">
	<body>
		The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
	</body>
</html>

examples.name: Failed Example 4
examples.description: This `html` element has no `lang` attribute, only a `xml:lang` attribute.
examples.code:
<html xml:lang="en">
	<body>
		The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
	</body>
</html>

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 1
examples.description: This rule does not apply to an `svg` element.
examples.code:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" lang="en">
    <text>
        The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
    </text>
</svg>

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 2
examples.description: This rule does not apply to a `math` element.
examples.code:
<math lang="en">
    The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
</math>

assumptions: The language of the page can be set by other methods than the `lang` attribute, for example using HTTP headers or the `meta` element. These methods are not supported by all assistive technologies. This rule assumes that these other methods are insufficient to satisfying [Success Criterion 3.1.1: Language of Page](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#language-of-page).
accessibility_support: There are no accessibility support issues known.
